content.content:
1	\paragraph{1996 B--2 \textnormal{[\texttt{asymptotic, integral-inequality}]}}
2	A reference asserts the following inequality. Determine whether the
3	assertion is correct.
4	
5	\textbf{Claim.} For every positive integer $n$,
6	\[
7	\left( \frac{2n+1}{e} \right)^{\frac{2n+1}{2}} \;<\;
8	1 \cdot 3 \cdot 5 \cdots (2n-1) \;<\;
9	\left( \frac{2n-1}{e} \right)^{\frac{2n-1}{2}}.
10	\]
11

content.text:
The plot clearly shows the asserted "lower bound" $((2n+1)/e)^{(2n+1)/2}$ is vastly LARGER than the asserted "upper bound" $((2n-1)/e)^{(2n-1)/2}$, making the inequality structurally impossible — the bounds are swapped.

```json
{
  "problem_id": "1996.B2",
  "variant": "false",
  "condition": "forced_visual",
  "verdict": "incorrect",
  "correct_answer": "((2n-1)/e)^{(2n-1)/2} < 1·3·5···(2n-1) < ((2n+1)/e)^{(2n+1)/2}",
  "viz_verdict": "The asserted lower bound ((2n+1)/e)^{(2n+1)/2} sits far ABOVE the asserted upper bound ((2n-1)/e)^{(2n-1)/2} for all n≥1, so no quantity can satisfy the stated double inequality — the two bounds are swapped.",
  "confidence": "high",
  "notes": "Spot-check at n=1 gives 1.159 < 1 < 0.607 (false); the genuine Putnam 1996 B2 inequality has the bounds in the opposite order, confirmed visually."
}
```
